explanation: Without TLB, every memory access requires two physical memory accesses: one to look up the page table and one for the actual data. TLB caches recent translations, so on a TLB hit, address translation is done in hardware without accessing main memory for the page table.

q: What happens to the TLB during a context switch?
options: TLB entries are shared between processes, no action needed
TLB is typically flushed (invalidated) since the new process has a different virtual address space
TLB automatically updates entries for the new process
TLB entries are saved to disk along with the process state
explanation: During a context switch, the TLB is typically flushed (all entries invalidated) because the new process has a different virtual-to-physical mapping. Some architectures use Address Space Identifiers (ASIDs) to tag TLB entries and avoid full flushes.

q: Which memory interleaving scheme is more effective for cache line prefetching and sequential access?
explanation: Low-order interleaving (bank interleaving) spreads consecutive addresses across different banks, maximizing parallelism for sequential access patterns. High-order interleaving keeps consecutive addresses in the same bank, offering less parallelism for sequential access.

q: The MESI protocol in cache coherence stands for:
explanation: MESI is a cache coherence protocol where each cache line can be in one of four states: Modified (dirty, only copy), Exclusive (clean, only copy), Shared (clean, may exist in other caches), or Invalid (not valid). It ensures consistency across multiple processor caches.

q: What is the typical access time relationship between L1, L2, and L3 caches?
explanation: L1 cache is the fastest (1-4 clock cycles), L2 is slower (4-12 cycles), and L3 is the slowest cache (20-40 cycles). This trade-off exists because L1 is smallest (16-64KB), L2 is larger (256KB-1MB), and L3 is the largest (4-32MB).

q: In a write-through cache with no write allocate, what happens on a write miss?
options: The block is loaded into cache and then written
The write is done directly to main memory without updating the cache
The write is buffered until the cache is full
explanation: With write-through + no-write-allocate (write-around) policy: on a write miss, data is written directly to main memory without loading the block into cache. This avoids polluting cache with data that may not be needed soon.

q: In a write-back cache with write allocate, what happens on a write miss?
options: Data is written directly to main memory
The block is first loaded from main memory into cache, then the write is performed in cache
The write is dropped and retried later
explanation: With write-back + write-allocate policy: on a write miss, the block is first fetched from main memory into cache (fetch-on-write), then the write is performed in cache. The dirty bit is set. Write-back naturally pairs with write-allocate for better performance.

q: How many bits are needed for the set index field in an address if a cache has 16 sets?
explanation: Number of bits for set index = logâ‚‚(Number of sets) = logâ‚‚(16) = 4 bits. These 4 bits from the address determine which of the 16 sets the block maps to.

q: If a cache block size is 64 bytes, how many bits are needed for the block offset field?
explanation: Block offset bits = logâ‚‚(Block size in bytes) = logâ‚‚(64) = 6 bits. These 6 bits identify the specific byte within a 64-byte cache block.

q: What is 'cache prefetching'?
options: Moving dirty cache lines to main memory before eviction
Loading data into cache before it is explicitly requested, based on predicted future accesses
Replacing cache lines based on future access prediction
Pre-allocating cache sets for specific processes
explanation: Cache prefetching loads data into cache before it is actually needed, based on access pattern prediction. Hardware prefetchers detect sequential or strided access patterns. Software prefetching uses explicit prefetch instructions. Prefetching hides memory latency.

q: Which of the following is a disadvantage of fully associative cache compared to direct-mapped cache?
options: Higher hardware complexity and cost due to parallel tag comparison
explanation: Fully associative cache requires comparing the tag of the incoming block with all cache line tags simultaneously (parallel comparators). This requires more hardware (comparators for every line) making it significantly more expensive and complex than direct-mapped cache.

q: In virtual memory, 'demand paging' means:
options: All pages of a process are loaded into memory before execution
Pages are loaded into memory only when they are actually needed (on access)
Pages are loaded based on predicted future access patterns
Pages are loaded in advance based on programmer hints
explanation: Demand paging loads pages into physical memory only when they are actually accessed â€” on demand. When a process accesses a page not in RAM, a page fault occurs, and the OS loads that page from disk. This reduces initial load time and memory usage.

q: What is the main advantage of write-back over write-through cache policy?
options: Reduced main memory bandwidth usage (fewer writes to memory)
explanation: Write-back reduces memory bandwidth by batching multiple writes to the same cache line into a single write to main memory (only on eviction). Write-through generates a memory write for every cache write, consuming more bandwidth even if the same location is written multiple times.

q: The 'Belady's anomaly' is associated with which page replacement algorithm?
explanation: Belady's anomaly occurs with FIFO page replacement: increasing the number of page frames can sometimes increase the number of page faults (counter-intuitive). LRU and OPT do not exhibit Belady's anomaly as they are stack algorithms.

q: What is the optimal cache replacement policy (Belady's algorithm)?
options: Replace the least recently used block
Replace the block that will not be used for the longest time in the future
Replace the block that was accessed the fewest times
Replace the oldest block in the cache
explanation: Belady's optimal algorithm replaces the block that will not be needed for the longest time in the future. It achieves the minimum possible miss rate but is impractical to implement (requires knowledge of future accesses). It serves as a theoretical benchmark.

q: In a system with 32-bit virtual addresses and 4 KB page size, how many entries does a single-level page table have?
explanation: Number of page table entries = Virtual address space / Page size = 2Â³Â² / 2Â¹Â² = 2Â²â° = 1,048,576 entries. With 32-bit addresses and 4KB (2Â¹Â²) pages, the virtual page number field is 20 bits, requiring 2Â²â° page table entries.

q: Memory interleaving with 'k' banks can theoretically reduce memory access time by a factor of:
explanation: With k-way memory interleaving, k consecutive memory accesses can be overlapped across k banks. In the ideal case (with pipeline overlap), effective memory bandwidth is multiplied by k, reducing the effective access time per request by a factor of k.

q: In a classic 5-stage pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB), how many stages are there?
explanation: The classic RISC pipeline has 5 stages: Instruction Fetch (IF), Instruction Decode (ID), Execute (EX), Memory Access (MEM), and Write Back (WB).

q: Which type of hazard occurs when two instructions attempt to use the same hardware resource at the same time?
explanation: A structural hazard arises when two or more instructions in the pipeline need the same hardware resource simultaneously, causing a conflict.

q: A RAW (Read After Write) dependency is an example of which hazard?
explanation: RAW (Read After Write) is a true data dependency and is classified as a data hazard. An instruction tries to read a register before a previous instruction has written to it.

q: Which technique eliminates data hazards by routing the result of an operation directly to the input of a subsequent instruction without waiting for WB stage?
explanation: Forwarding (also called bypassing or short-circuiting) passes the computed result directly from the EX or MEM stage output to the input of the EX stage of a dependent instruction, avoiding stalls.

q: A pipeline stall (bubble) is also known as:
explanation: A pipeline interlock (or pipeline stall/bubble) is the mechanism that detects hazards and delays pipeline stages by inserting NOPs (no-operations) until the hazard is resolved.

q: In a 5-stage pipeline with no forwarding, a RAW hazard on a register typically causes how many stall cycles?
explanation: Without forwarding, the dependent instruction must wait until the producing instruction completes WB. This causes 2 stall cycles (bubbles) in a standard 5-stage pipeline.

q: Which hazard is caused by branch instructions in a pipeline?
explanation: Control hazards (also called branch hazards) occur because the pipeline fetches subsequent instructions before knowing the outcome and target of a branch instruction.

q: What is the CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) of an ideal pipeline with no hazards?
explanation: An ideal pipeline with no hazards achieves a CPI of 1, meaning one instruction completes per clock cycle in steady state.

q: The WAW (Write After Write) hazard is also called:
explanation: WAW (Write After Write) is called an output dependence because both instructions write to the same register, and the final value depends on which writes last.

q: WAR (Write After Read) hazard is also known as:
explanation: WAR (Write After Read) is called an anti-dependence because it is not a true data dependence but arises from reuse of register names.

q: Branch prediction that always predicts the branch as not taken is called:
explanation: Always predicting not-taken or always predicting taken are examples of static branch prediction, as they do not change based on runtime history.

q: A 2-bit saturating counter used in branch prediction can be in how many states?
explanation: A 2-bit saturating counter has 4 states: Strongly Not Taken, Weakly Not Taken, Weakly Taken, Strongly Taken. It provides hysteresis to avoid mispredictions on occasional anomalies.

q: Which component stores branch history to assist dynamic branch prediction?
explanation: The Branch Target Buffer (BTB) is a cache-like structure that stores the target addresses and prediction state of recently executed branch instructions.

q: Superscalar processors can issue:
explanation: Superscalar processors have multiple execution units and can issue more than one instruction per clock cycle, achieving CPI < 1 (IPC > 1).

q: In RISC architecture, instructions are typically:
explanation: RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) uses fixed-length, simple instructions that execute in a single clock cycle and operate primarily on registers.

q: CISC architecture is characterized by:
options: Complex instructions that can perform multi-step operations
explanation: CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) has variable-length, complex instructions that can perform multi-step operations (e.g., memory access + arithmetic) in a single instruction.

q: Which architecture uses a load-store model where only load/store instructions access memory?
explanation: RISC uses a load-store architecture: only dedicated LOAD and STORE instructions access memory, while all other operations work on registers.

q: VLIW stands for:
explanation: VLIW stands for Very Long Instruction Word. It encodes multiple operations in a single wide instruction word, with parallelism determined at compile time by the compiler.

q: In VLIW architecture, who is responsible for identifying instruction-level parallelism?
explanation: In VLIW, the compiler is responsible for statically scheduling instructions and packing multiple independent operations into a single VLIW instruction word.

q: Out-of-order execution allows a processor to:
options: Execute instructions only in program order
Execute instructions in an order different from program order to avoid stalls
explanation: Out-of-order execution (OOO) allows the processor to execute instructions in an order different from the original program order to better utilize execution units and avoid stalls on long-latency operations.

q: The Tomasulo algorithm was originally developed for which machine?
explanation: The Tomasulo algorithm was developed by Robert Tomasulo at IBM for the IBM System/360 Model 91 floating-point unit in 1967.

q: In the Tomasulo algorithm, reservation stations are used to:
options: Buffer instructions waiting for operands and track dependencies
explanation: Reservation stations in the Tomasulo algorithm buffer instructions that are waiting for their operands. They track which operands are available and which are still being computed.

q: The Tomasulo algorithm eliminates WAR and WAW hazards through:
explanation: The Tomasulo algorithm uses register renaming (via reservation station tags) to eliminate WAR and WAW false dependencies, allowing more instructions to execute in parallel.

q: In the Tomasulo algorithm, results are broadcast to all waiting reservation stations via:
explanation: The Common Data Bus (CDB) broadcasts computed results to all reservation stations simultaneously. Any reservation station waiting for that result can capture it immediately.

q: Which of the following is NOT a stage in the classic 5-stage RISC pipeline?
explanation: The 5 classic stages are IF, ID, EX, MEM, and WB. 'Branch Resolve' is not a standard stage name, though branch resolution happens during EX or MEM stage.

q: Pipeline throughput is maximized when:
options: The first stage is the slowest
The last stage is the slowest
explanation: Pipeline throughput is maximized when all stages have equal latency. The clock period is determined by the slowest stage, so balanced stages minimize the clock period and maximize throughput.

q: The speedup of a pipeline with k stages over a non-pipelined processor for n instructions approaches:
explanation: As n â†’ âˆž, the speedup of a k-stage pipeline approaches k (the number of pipeline stages), as the startup latency becomes negligible compared to total execution time.

q: Delayed branching is a technique where:
options: One or more instructions after a branch always execute regardless of branch outcome
Branches are always predicted not taken
Branches stall the pipeline for extra cycles
explanation: In delayed branching, the instruction(s) in the branch delay slot always execute after the branch instruction, regardless of whether the branch is taken or not. The compiler fills these slots with useful work.

q: Which hazard can be solved by duplicating hardware resources?
explanation: Structural hazards due to resource conflicts can be resolved by duplicating hardware resources (e.g., having separate instruction and data caches to avoid memory access conflicts).

q: A pipeline with forwarding can resolve most RAW hazards EXCEPT those caused by:
options: Load-use hazards (load followed immediately by a dependent instruction)
explanation: A load-use hazard occurs when a LOAD instruction is immediately followed by an instruction that uses the loaded value. Since the data is not available until after the MEM stage, even forwarding cannot avoid a 1-cycle stall.

q: In dynamic branch prediction, a branch history table is indexed by:
options: Lower-order bits of the program counter (PC)
explanation: A branch history table (BHT) or pattern history table is typically indexed using lower-order bits of the PC to look up the prediction for that branch.

q: Register renaming in out-of-order processors primarily helps eliminate:
explanation: Register renaming maps architectural registers to a larger set of physical registers, eliminating false dependencies (WAR and WAW hazards) while preserving true RAW dependencies.

q: The Reorder Buffer (ROB) in out-of-order processors is used to:
options: Ensure instructions commit in program order despite out-of-order execution
explanation: The Reorder Buffer (ROB) holds instructions that have been issued but not yet committed. It allows in-order commit of results, maintaining precise exceptions and correct program semantics.

q: In RISC processors, the number of general-purpose registers is typically:
explanation: RISC processors typically have 32 or more general-purpose registers to support the load-store architecture and reduce memory access frequency.

q: Which of the following best describes 'speculative execution'?
options: Executing instructions before it is certain they are needed (e.g., past a branch)
Delaying execution until all operands are ready
Executing the same instruction twice for verification
explanation: Speculative execution allows the processor to execute instructions before knowing whether they will actually be needed (e.g., along a predicted branch path), improving performance but requiring rollback on misprediction.

q: What is IPC in the context of superscalar processors?
explanation: IPC stands for Instructions Per Cycle. Superscalar processors achieve IPC > 1 by issuing and executing multiple instructions simultaneously in each clock cycle.

q: In a VLIW processor, if not all slots in a VLIW instruction can be filled with useful operations, the empty slots are filled with:
explanation: When the compiler cannot find enough independent operations to fill all slots of a VLIW instruction word, it inserts NOP (no-operation) instructions in the empty slots, which wastes instruction word bits.

q: Which of the following is an advantage of RISC over CISC?
options: Easier to pipeline due to fixed-length, uniform instructions
Built-in support for complex addressing modes
explanation: RISC's fixed-length, simple, and uniform instructions make it easier to pipeline efficiently. CISC instructions vary in length and complexity, complicating pipeline design.

q: Control hazards in a pipeline are caused by:
options: Branches and jumps that change the PC unpredictably
explanation: Control hazards arise from branch and jump instructions that change the program counter. The pipeline has already fetched subsequent instructions that may not be the correct next instructions.

q: In Tomasulo's algorithm, an instruction is said to be in 'issue' stage when:
options: It is dispatched to a reservation station if one is free
Its result is written to the register file
explanation: In Tomasulo's algorithm, the issue stage dispatches an instruction from the instruction queue to a free reservation station, performing register renaming at this point.

q: The term 'pipeline flush' refers to:
options: Discarding all instructions currently in the pipeline (e.g., after a branch misprediction)
Stalling the pipeline for one cycle
explanation: A pipeline flush (or squash) discards all instructions currently in the pipeline stages, typically after a branch misprediction or an exception, to start fetching from the correct address.

q: Superscalar execution differs from VLIW in that:
options: Superscalar uses compiler-scheduled parallelism; VLIW uses hardware-scheduled parallelism
Superscalar uses hardware to detect and exploit ILP at runtime; VLIW relies on the compiler
explanation: Superscalar processors use hardware schedulers to detect instruction-level parallelism at runtime, while VLIW relies on the compiler to statically schedule parallel operations into wide instruction words.

q: Which of the following correctly describes a 'structural hazard' example in a single-memory pipeline?
options: Two instructions write to the same register
A load instruction and an instruction fetch both need memory access in the same cycle
A branch instruction changes the PC
An ALU instruction uses a stale register value
explanation: A classic structural hazard occurs in a single-memory design when a MEM-stage instruction accesses data memory while the IF stage tries to fetch the next instructionâ€”both require the same memory resource.

q: How does 'loop unrolling' help instruction-level parallelism?
options: Reduces the number of loop iterations
Exposes more independent instructions that can be scheduled in parallel
explanation: Loop unrolling replicates the loop body multiple times, exposing more independent instructions. This allows the compiler or hardware to schedule them in parallel, increasing ILP.

q: In the context of branch prediction, what is 'branch misprediction penalty'?
options: Number of pipeline stages that must be flushed/refilled after an incorrect prediction
Number of branch instructions in a program
Memory bandwidth wasted on wrong fetches
explanation: The branch misprediction penalty is the number of pipeline cycles wasted because instructions fetched along the wrong path must be flushed and execution must restart from the correct path.

q: Which pipeline stage performs the actual computation (e.g., ADD, SUB) in the classic 5-stage pipeline?
explanation: The EX (Execute) stage performs the actual arithmetic or logical computation using the ALU, as well as address calculation for memory instructions.

q: An instruction that reads a register written by a preceding instruction has a _____ dependency.
explanation: RAW (Read After Write) means a later instruction reads a register that an earlier instruction has written. This is a true data dependency and is the most common type of data hazard.

q: Which of the following techniques does NOT directly address control hazards?
explanation: Data forwarding addresses data hazards (RAW), not control hazards. Branch prediction, delayed branching, and reducing branch frequency via loop unrolling all help mitigate control hazards.

q: In Tomasulo's algorithm, when an execution unit completes, the result is placed on the Common Data Bus (CDB). What happens next?
options: The result is written only to the destination register
All reservation stations and the register file snoop the CDB and capture the result if they are waiting for it
The result is stored in the TLB
explanation: When a result is broadcast on the CDB, all reservation stations monitoring the CDB capture it if they were waiting for that tag, and the register file (or ROB) also captures the result â€” enabling waiting instructions to proceed.

q: Which of the following is a key difference between RISC and CISC regarding instruction encoding?
options: RISC uses variable-length encoding; CISC uses fixed-length
RISC uses fixed-length encoding; CISC uses variable-length
explanation: RISC uses fixed-length instruction encoding (e.g., all 32 bits), simplifying decoding and pipeline design. CISC uses variable-length instructions (e.g., x86 instructions range from 1 to 15 bytes), complicating decoding.

q: Which I/O interface transmits data one bit at a time over a single wire?
explanation: A serial interface transmits data one bit at a time sequentially over a single communication line, unlike a parallel interface which sends multiple bits simultaneously.

q: In programmed I/O, the CPU checks the status of an I/O device repeatedly in a loop. This technique is called:
explanation: Polling (busy-wait I/O) is the technique in programmed I/O where the CPU continuously checks (polls) the I/O device's status register in a loop until the device is ready, wasting CPU cycles.

q: Which I/O technique completely frees the CPU from managing data transfer between memory and an I/O device?
explanation: DMA (Direct Memory Access) allows the DMA controller to transfer data directly between memory and I/O devices without CPU intervention, freeing the CPU for other tasks during the transfer.

q: In interrupt-driven I/O, the CPU is interrupted after:
options: The I/O device completes an operation and is ready
explanation: In interrupt-driven I/O, the I/O device sends an interrupt signal to the CPU when it is ready (e.g., data received or sent), allowing the CPU to perform other tasks in between and only respond when needed.

q: Which register in a DMA controller specifies the starting memory address for a data transfer?
explanation: The address register (also called the memory address register) in a DMA controller holds the starting address in main memory where data will be read from or written to during the DMA transfer.

q: DMA controller requests the bus from the CPU using a signal called:
explanation: The DMA controller asserts the Bus Request (BR) signal to the CPU when it needs to take control of the system bus. The CPU responds with a Bus Grant (BG) signal when it releases the bus.

q: The mode in which the DMA controller transfers an entire block of data before releasing the bus is called:
explanation: In burst mode (block transfer mode), the DMA controller holds the bus and transfers the entire block of data at once before releasing the bus back to the CPU, maximizing throughput but blocking CPU bus access temporarily.

q: In cycle stealing DMA, the DMA controller:
options: Transfers all data in one burst
Steals one bus cycle at a time from the CPU
Operates only when the CPU is idle
Uses a dedicated bus separate from the CPU
explanation: In cycle stealing mode, the DMA controller steals individual bus cycles from the CPU one at a time to transfer single words, then releases the bus back. This interleaves DMA transfers with CPU operations.

q: A non-maskable interrupt (NMI) differs from a maskable interrupt in that:
options: NMI can be disabled by software
NMI cannot be disabled and must always be serviced
NMI has lower priority than maskable interrupts
NMI is generated only by software
explanation: A non-maskable interrupt (NMI) cannot be disabled or ignored by the CPU through software (e.g., by clearing the interrupt enable flag). It is always processed and is typically used for critical events like hardware failures or power-fail detection.

q: A software interrupt is generated by:
options: An I/O device asserting an IRQ line
A specific instruction in the program (e.g., INT instruction)
The DMA controller completing a transfer
explanation: Software interrupts are generated by executing a specific interrupt instruction (like INT n in x86), used for system calls and trap handling. They are also called traps or exceptions and originate from the program itself.

q: Which of the following is an example of a hardware interrupt?
explanation: A hardware interrupt is triggered by an external hardware device (like a keyboard, mouse, or timer) via the interrupt request (IRQ) lines. A keyboard key press generates a hardware interrupt to notify the CPU.

q: The routine executed by the CPU in response to an interrupt is called:
explanation: An Interrupt Service Routine (ISR), also called an interrupt handler, is the specific code executed by the CPU when it responds to an interrupt. After saving context, the CPU jumps to the ISR address found in the interrupt vector table.

q: Before executing an Interrupt Service Routine, the CPU saves the current execution context primarily in:
explanation: Before jumping to the ISR, the CPU automatically pushes the Program Counter (PC/return address) and status flags onto the stack. This allows the CPU to resume the interrupted program after the ISR completes.

q: The table that stores the starting addresses of all Interrupt Service Routines is called the:
explanation: The interrupt vector table (IVT) is a data structure that maps each interrupt number (vector) to the starting address (vector) of its corresponding ISR. In x86 real mode, the IVT is located at memory address 0x0000.

q: In daisy chaining for interrupt priority, the device with the highest priority is:
options: Connected closest to the CPU in the chain
Selected randomly by the interrupt controller
explanation: In a daisy chain interrupt scheme, the interrupt acknowledge signal propagates from the CPU through devices in series. The device closest to the CPU receives the acknowledge signal first, giving it the highest priority.

q: Which bus carries the memory addresses generated by the CPU?
explanation: The address bus carries the memory or I/O port addresses generated by the CPU to specify the location it wants to read from or write to. It is unidirectional (from CPU to memory/I/O).

q: The width of the address bus determines:
options: The maximum amount of addressable memory
The number of I/O devices supported
The word size of the CPU
explanation: The address bus width (in bits) determines the maximum addressable memory space. A 32-bit address bus can address 2^32 = 4 GB of memory, while a 64-bit address bus can address 2^64 bytes.

q: Which bus carries signals like Read, Write, Memory Select, and I/O Select?
explanation: The control bus carries control signals that coordinate and regulate the activities on the other buses. Signals like Memory Read, Memory Write, I/O Read, I/O Write, Bus Request, Bus Grant, and Interrupt signals travel on the control bus.

q: The data bus is bidirectional because:
options: The CPU both reads data from and writes data to memory/I/O
It carries both data and control signals
It is shared among multiple CPUs
explanation: The data bus is bidirectional because data flows from memory/I/O to the CPU during read operations and from the CPU to memory/I/O during write operations. Both directions use the same physical bus lines.

q: Bus arbitration is needed in a computer system because:
options: The CPU needs to synchronize with external clocks
Multiple bus masters (CPU, DMA) may compete for bus ownership simultaneously
The data bus and address bus must be isolated
explanation: Bus arbitration is required when multiple devices capable of initiating bus transfers (bus masters), such as the CPU and DMA controllers, need to use the shared bus. Arbitration determines which device gets bus access at any given time.

q: In centralized bus arbitration, a single component called the _______ decides which device gets bus access.
explanation: In centralized bus arbitration, a dedicated bus arbiter (often part of the chipset or a separate chip) receives bus requests from all potential bus masters and grants bus access to one device at a time based on a priority scheme.

q: Which bus arbitration scheme gives each device equal average access to the bus, rotating priority among requesters?
explanation: Round-robin arbitration rotates priority among all requesting devices in a circular order, ensuring each device gets equal average bus access time and preventing starvation of low-priority devices.

q: USB stands for:
explanation: USB stands for Universal Serial Bus. It is an industry-standard serial bus interface for connecting peripheral devices to a computer, supporting plug-and-play and hot-swapping capabilities.

q: The maximum data transfer rate of USB 2.0 (High Speed) is:
explanation: USB 2.0 High Speed mode supports a maximum data transfer rate of 480 Mbps (megabits per second). USB 2.0 also supports Full Speed (12 Mbps) and Low Speed (1.5 Mbps) modes.

q: In USB architecture, which device controls all communication on the USB bus?
explanation: The USB host controller manages all communication on the USB bus. It initiates all transfers, manages the USB protocol, and interfaces between the system bus and the USB bus. There is only one host controller per USB bus.

q: A parallel interface transfers data:
options: One bit at a time over a single line
Multiple bits simultaneously over multiple lines
Using differential signaling over two lines
explanation: A parallel interface transmits multiple bits simultaneously, with each bit having its own dedicated line. For example, an 8-bit parallel interface has 8 data lines plus control lines, transferring one byte per clock cycle.

q: The main disadvantage of parallel interfaces over long distances is:
options: Skew â€” bits arriving at different times due to slight timing differences between lines
explanation: At high speeds or long distances, parallel interfaces suffer from skew â€” the phenomenon where bits sent simultaneously arrive at slightly different times due to minor differences in wire lengths or electrical characteristics, causing data corruption.

q: RS-232 is an example of which type of I/O interface standard?
explanation: RS-232 (Recommended Standard 232) is a standard for serial communication, defining voltage levels, signal timing, and connector types for asynchronous serial data transmission between DTE and DCE devices.

q: In memory-mapped I/O, I/O devices are accessed using:
options: The same address space as main memory using regular memory instructions
Dedicated I/O bus separate from the memory bus
explanation: In memory-mapped I/O, I/O device registers are mapped into the same address space as main memory. The CPU uses the same load/store (read/write) instructions to access both memory and I/O devices, distinguished only by their addresses.

q: In isolated I/O (port-mapped I/O), I/O devices have:
options: Addresses within the main memory address space
A separate address space accessed via special IN/OUT instructions
No addresses; they use interrupts exclusively
Addresses in the upper half of the memory address space
explanation: In isolated I/O (also called port-mapped I/O), I/O devices use a separate address space from main memory. Special instructions (IN and OUT in x86) are required to access I/O ports, which have their own 16-bit address space in x86.

q: What is the primary purpose of an interrupt priority scheme?
options: To ensure more critical interrupts are handled before less critical ones
explanation: Interrupt priority schemes ensure that when multiple interrupts occur simultaneously, the most critical (highest priority) interrupt is serviced first. This is essential for real-time systems where some events require immediate attention.

q: A maskable interrupt can be:
options: Enabled or disabled by the CPU through software by setting/clearing an interrupt enable flag
explanation: Maskable interrupts can be enabled or disabled by the CPU through software. By clearing the interrupt enable flag (e.g., CLI instruction in x86), the CPU can temporarily ignore maskable interrupts during critical code sections.

q: The INTR (Interrupt Request) pin in x86 processors receives:
options: Maskable interrupt signals from the Programmable Interrupt Controller (PIC)
explanation: The INTR pin on x86 processors receives maskable hardware interrupt requests, typically from the 8259A Programmable Interrupt Controller (PIC) which aggregates multiple device IRQ lines into a single signal to the CPU.

q: The NMI pin in x86 processors is used for:
options: Critical events like memory parity errors and hardware failures
explanation: The NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) pin on x86 processors is connected to critical hardware events like memory parity errors, bus errors, or power failure detection circuits that require immediate CPU attention regardless of the interrupt flag state.

q: After completing an ISR, the instruction used to return to the interrupted program is:
explanation: IRET (Interrupt Return) is used to return from an ISR. Unlike a regular RET, IRET also restores the processor status flags (from the stack) in addition to the return address, fully restoring the CPU state at the point of interruption.

q: In the daisy chain interrupt scheme, if Device 2 and Device 4 simultaneously request an interrupt, which gets serviced first (assuming Device 1 is closest to CPU)?
options: Device 4, as it has a higher number
Device 2, as it is closer to the CPU in the chain
The device that requested first in time
explanation: In daisy chaining, the interrupt acknowledge (INTA) signal travels serially from the CPU through Device 1, Device 2, etc. Device 2 intercepts the INTA signal before it reaches Device 4, so Device 2 (closer to CPU) gets higher priority.

q: USB supports hot-plugging, which means:
options: The bus operates at high temperatures
Devices can be connected/disconnected while the system is running without rebooting
The USB hub provides power to connected devices
Data is transmitted in hot-standby mode
explanation: Hot-plugging (hot-swapping) means USB devices can be physically connected or disconnected from the bus while the computer is powered on and running. The OS automatically detects and configures the device without requiring a system restart.

q: The maximum number of devices that can be connected to a single USB host controller (including hubs) is:
explanation: USB supports up to 127 devices per host controller. Each device is assigned a unique 7-bit address (2^7 = 128, but address 0 is reserved for enumeration), allowing addresses 1-127 for connected devices.

q: Which DMA transfer mode is most transparent to the CPU because it only uses memory cycles that the CPU is not using?
explanation: In transparent mode (also called interleaved or time-sharing mode), the DMA controller transfers data only during memory cycles when the CPU is not using the bus (e.g., during internal CPU operations). This is most transparent but complex to implement.

q: The control bus signal that indicates the CPU is performing a memory read operation is:
explanation: The MEMR (Memory Read) signal on the control bus indicates that the CPU is performing a read operation from memory. The address bus simultaneously carries the memory address being read.

q: In a system with a 16-bit data bus, how many bytes can be transferred in a single bus cycle?
explanation: A 16-bit data bus has 16 lines and can transfer 16 bits (2 bytes) simultaneously in a single bus cycle. The bus width directly determines the amount of data that can be transferred per bus transaction.

q: Which of the following best describes a bus master?
options: A device that only responds to bus transactions
A device capable of initiating bus transactions and taking control of the bus
A device that monitors bus traffic
explanation: A bus master is any device capable of initiating bus transactions â€” it can request bus ownership and drive the address and control buses. The CPU is a bus master, but so are DMA controllers and some intelligent I/O controllers.

q: Vectored interrupts differ from non-vectored interrupts in that:
options: The interrupting device supplies a vector (address/index) identifying its ISR directly
Vectored interrupts do not save the return address
explanation: In vectored interrupts, the interrupting device places a vector (interrupt number or address) on the data bus during the acknowledge cycle, allowing the CPU to jump directly to the correct ISR. Non-vectored interrupts always jump to a fixed address.

q: The 8259A Programmable Interrupt Controller (PIC) in the original IBM PC manages how many IRQ lines?
explanation: The 8259A PIC manages 8 IRQ lines (IR0â€“IR7). In the IBM PC/AT, two 8259A chips are cascaded (master + slave) to provide 15 usable IRQ lines (IRQ 0â€“7 and IRQ 8â€“15, with IRQ 2 used for cascade).

q: USB 3.0 (SuperSpeed) has a maximum data transfer rate of:
explanation: USB 3.0 (SuperSpeed USB) supports a maximum data transfer rate of 5 Gbps (gigabits per second), which is approximately 10 times faster than USB 2.0 High Speed (480 Mbps).

q: In distributed bus arbitration, priority is determined:
options: By a single centralized arbiter chip
By each device examining bus request signals from all other devices and deciding locally
By the order in which devices are physically connected
By the operating system through software priority assignments
explanation: In distributed bus arbitration, there is no central arbiter. Each device has arbitration logic that monitors the bus request signals from all other devices and uses a predefined algorithm (e.g., each device has a unique priority ID) to determine locally whether it has the highest priority.

q: Which type of interrupt is triggered by an abnormal condition in the currently executing instruction, such as division by zero?
explanation: A trap or exception is a synchronous interrupt triggered by an abnormal condition detected during instruction execution, such as division by zero, invalid opcode, overflow, or page fault. It is synchronous because it is directly caused by the executing instruction.

q: In the context of I/O ports, the term 'port' refers to:
options: A physical connector on the motherboard
A register within an I/O device interface through which the CPU communicates with the device
A dedicated interrupt line for an I/O device
A buffer in the DMA controller
explanation: An I/O port is a register within the I/O device's interface (controller) that the CPU reads from or writes to in order to communicate with the device. Ports include data registers, status registers, and control registers.

q: The advantage of interrupt-driven I/O over programmed I/O (polling) is:
options: The CPU does not waste cycles waiting; it performs other tasks until the device signals readiness
explanation: Interrupt-driven I/O improves CPU efficiency significantly. Instead of busy-waiting in a polling loop (wasting cycles checking device status), the CPU performs useful work and is only interrupted when the I/O device actually needs service.

q: USB uses which topology for connecting devices?
explanation: USB uses a tiered star topology. The host controller connects to a root hub, which connects to USB hubs or devices. Each hub creates a new tier of connections, forming a tree structure with up to 5 levels of hubs (7 tiers total including root).

q: When the DMA controller completes a block transfer, it typically notifies the CPU by:
options: Writing a completion code to memory
Sending a message over the data bus
explanation: Upon completing a DMA block transfer, the DMA controller asserts an interrupt request (IRQ) line to notify the CPU that the transfer is complete. The CPU's ISR can then process the transferred data or initiate the next transfer.

q: In binary addition, what is the result of 1011 + 1101?
What is the 2's complement representation of -13 in 8-bit format?
explanation: 13 in binary is 00001101. 1's complement is 11110010. Adding 1 gives 11110011. So -13 in 2's complement (8-bit) is 11110011.

q: In binary subtraction using 2's complement, what is 1010 - 0011?
explanation: 0011's 2's complement is 1101. Adding: 1010 + 1101 = 10111. Ignoring carry (overflow bit), result is 0111 (7). 1010(10) - 0011(3) = 7.

q: Booth's algorithm is used for:
explanation: Booth's algorithm is an efficient method for multiplying two signed binary integers using 2's complement representation, reducing the number of partial products.

q: In Booth's algorithm, when the current bit and previous bit are '10', the operation performed is:
explanation: In Booth's encoding: '10' (transition from 1 to 0, i.e., end of a run of 1s) means subtract the multiplicand from the accumulator, then arithmetic right shift.

q: In Booth's algorithm, when the bit pair (current, previous) is '01', the operation is:
explanation: Bit pair '01' (transition from 0 to 1, i.e., beginning of a run of 1s) means add the multiplicand to the partial product accumulator.

q: In the restoring division algorithm, what happens when the partial remainder becomes negative after subtraction?
options: The divisor is added back to restore the remainder
The quotient bit is set to 1
explanation: In restoring division, if the trial subtraction yields a negative result (sign bit = 1), the divisor is added back to restore the partial remainder, and the quotient bit is set to 0.

q: Non-restoring division differs from restoring division in that:
options: It avoids restoring the remainder after a negative trial subtraction
It only works for unsigned numbers
explanation: Non-restoring division skips the restoration step. If the partial remainder is negative, instead of restoring it, the next step adds the divisor (instead of subtracting), saving one addition per step.

q: In IEEE 754 single-precision floating point, how many bits are used for the exponent?
explanation: IEEE 754 single-precision (32-bit) format uses 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, and 23 mantissa (significand) bits.

q: The bias value used in IEEE 754 single-precision floating point exponent is:
explanation: IEEE 754 single-precision uses a biased exponent with bias = 127 (= 2^(8-1) - 1). The actual exponent = stored exponent - 127.

q: In IEEE 754 double-precision floating point, the exponent bias is:
explanation: IEEE 754 double-precision (64-bit) uses 11 exponent bits. The bias = 2^(11-1) - 1 = 1023.

q: Overflow in signed integer arithmetic occurs when:
options: The result exceeds the representable range of the data type
There is a carry out of the most significant bit
explanation: Overflow occurs when the arithmetic result cannot be represented in the allocated number of bits, i.e., the result exceeds the range [âˆ’2^(nâˆ’1), 2^(nâˆ’1)âˆ’1] for n-bit signed integers.

q: Overflow in 2's complement addition can be detected when:
options: The carry into the sign bit differs from the carry out of the sign bit
explanation: Overflow in 2's complement is detected when carry-in to the MSB (sign bit) â‰  carry-out from the MSB. This indicates the sign of the result is incorrect.

q: Underflow in floating point arithmetic refers to:
options: A result too small to be represented (magnitude less than smallest normal number)
A result too large to be represented
explanation: Floating point underflow occurs when a non-zero result's magnitude is smaller than the smallest representable normal number, potentially resulting in a denormalized number or zero.

q: Guard bits in floating point arithmetic are used to:
options: Improve rounding accuracy by retaining extra bits during intermediate computations
explanation: Guard bits are extra bits maintained during floating point operations beyond the normal precision. They help in producing correctly rounded results by retaining information about bits that would otherwise be lost.

q: In IEEE 754 floating point, the 'round to nearest, ties to even' (round half to even) rounding mode is also called:
explanation: Round half to even (banker's rounding) rounds to the nearest value; if exactly halfway, rounds to the nearest even number. It minimizes cumulative rounding error in statistical computations.

q: Which IEEE 754 rounding mode always rounds toward positive infinity?
explanation: The 'round toward +âˆž' mode (ceiling function) always rounds to the next higher representable value. It is one of the four IEEE 754 rounding modes.

q: BCD (Binary Coded Decimal) represents each decimal digit using:
explanation: BCD encodes each decimal digit (0â€“9) using 4 binary bits. For example, decimal 9 is represented as 1001 in BCD.

q: When adding two BCD digits and the result exceeds 9 (or a carry is generated), what correction is applied?
explanation: If BCD addition of two digits yields a value > 9 (1001) or produces a carry, adding 6 (0110) adjusts the result back into valid BCD representation (0â€“9 per nibble).

q: What is the BCD representation of decimal 47?
explanation: In BCD, each digit is encoded separately: 4 â†’ 0100, 7 â†’ 0111. So 47 in BCD is 0100 0111.

q: Carry propagation delay in a ripple carry adder is:
options: O(n) where n is the number of bits
explanation: In a ripple carry adder, carry propagates sequentially from the LSB to the MSB. The worst-case delay grows linearly with the number of bits, making it O(n).

q: A carry lookahead adder (CLA) reduces carry propagation delay to:
explanation: Carry lookahead adder computes carry signals in parallel using generate (G) and propagate (P) signals, reducing the carry delay to O(log n) compared to O(n) in ripple carry adder.

q: In carry lookahead logic, the generate function G_i is defined as:
explanation: The generate function G_i = A_i Â· B_i means bit position i generates a carry regardless of incoming carry. The propagate P_i = A_i âŠ• B_i.

q: Saturating arithmetic differs from regular (wrapping) arithmetic in that:
options: Results are clamped to the maximum or minimum representable value on overflow
Results wrap around modulo 2^n on overflow
explanation: In saturating arithmetic, when a result overflows the maximum value it stays at MAX, and when it underflows below minimum it stays at MIN. This is used in DSP and multimedia to avoid wrap-around distortions.

q: In 4-bit saturating unsigned arithmetic, what is 14 + 5?
explanation: 4-bit unsigned max is 15. 14 + 5 = 19, which exceeds 15. In saturating arithmetic the result is clamped to 15 (the maximum), not wrapped to 3.

q: Booth's algorithm in its basic form uses a recoding that reduces the number of partial products by replacing strings of 1s with:
options: +1 at the start and -1 at the end of the run
explanation: Booth's algorithm recodes a string of k consecutive 1s (e.g., 0111...10) as +1 followed by -1 at the boundaries, replacing k additions with just 2 operations (one add, one subtract).

q: How many bits are in the mantissa (significand, excluding the hidden bit) of IEEE 754 double-precision format?
explanation: IEEE 754 double-precision (64-bit) has 1 sign bit, 11 exponent bits, and 52 explicit mantissa bits. The leading 1 (hidden/implied bit) gives 53 bits of effective precision.

q: The hidden/implicit bit in IEEE 754 normalized floating point numbers refers to:
options: The leading 1 of the significand that is not explicitly stored
The least significant bit of the mantissa
explanation: Since all normalized IEEE 754 numbers have the form 1.xxx...x Ã— 2^e, the leading 1 is implied and not stored, giving one extra bit of precision for free.

q: What is the IEEE 754 special representation for positive infinity in single precision?
explanation: +âˆž in IEEE 754 single precision: sign = 0, exponent = 11111111 (255), mantissa = 00...0. NaN has exponent = 255 with non-zero mantissa.

q: A denormalized (subnormal) number in IEEE 754 is characterized by:
options: Exponent field all zeros and non-zero mantissa
Exponent field all ones and non-zero mantissa
Exponent field all zeros and zero mantissa
Exponent field all ones and zero mantissa
explanation: Denormalized numbers have exponent = 0 and non-zero mantissa. They represent very small values near zero, filling the gap between zero and the smallest normal number. The implied leading bit is 0, not 1.

q: In non-restoring division, when the current partial remainder is negative, the next step is to:
options: Shift left and add the divisor
Shift left and subtract the divisor
explanation: In non-restoring division: if partial remainder R < 0, set quotient bit to 0 and compute next step as R_new = 2R + D (shift left and add divisor). If R â‰¥ 0, set quotient bit to 1 and compute R_new = 2R - D.

q: The number of steps required in the restoring division algorithm to divide an n-bit dividend by an n-bit divisor is:
explanation: Restoring (and non-restoring) division requires exactly n iterations to produce an n-bit quotient when dividing an n-bit dividend by an n-bit divisor.

q: In IEEE 754 floating point addition, before adding two numbers, they must be:
options: Aligned by shifting the smaller exponent's mantissa right
explanation: To add two floating point numbers, the exponents must be equal. The number with the smaller exponent has its mantissa right-shifted (and exponent incremented) until both exponents are equal â€” called alignment.

q: After floating point addition/subtraction, the result is renormalized. If the result has the form 1X.xxx (two bits before binary point), the mantissa is:
options: Right-shifted by 1 and exponent incremented by 1
Left-shifted by 1 and exponent decremented by 1
Left-shifted until leading 1 is in the units place
explanation: If the addition produces a carry shifting the result to 1X.xxx format, the mantissa is right-shifted by 1 position and the exponent is incremented by 1 to renormalize to the 1.xxx form.

q: Which of the following correctly describes 'round toward zero' (truncation) in IEEE 754?
options: Extra bits are simply discarded (magnitude is reduced toward zero)
The result is rounded to the nearest even
The result is always rounded up
Extra bits are set to one
explanation: Round toward zero (truncation) simply drops the extra bits. For positive numbers this rounds down, for negative numbers this rounds up â€” always reducing the magnitude toward zero.

q: The sticky bit in floating point rounding is used to:
options: Indicate whether any bits beyond the guard bit were non-zero
Store the sign of the result
explanation: The sticky bit is the OR of all bits shifted out beyond the round bit. It is used together with guard and round bits to determine the correct rounding direction, particularly for round-to-nearest.

q: In carry save addition (used in multipliers), the main advantage is:
options: Carries are saved for later resolution, allowing multiple operands to be added without carry propagation
It eliminates the need for a final carry propagate adder
It works only for unsigned numbers
It reduces the number of bits per operand
explanation: Carry-save adders (CSA) process three inputs to produce two outputs (sum and carry) without propagating carries. This allows fast summation of many partial products in multipliers, with only one final carry-propagate addition.

q: Overflow in unsigned binary addition is indicated by:
options: A carry out from the most significant bit position
No carry out from the MSB
All bits of the result being zero
explanation: In unsigned addition, a carry out from the MSB means the result exceeds 2^n - 1 (the maximum n-bit unsigned value), indicating overflow.

q: In 2's complement representation, adding two positive numbers and getting a negative result indicates:
explanation: If two positive numbers are added and the result has the sign bit set (1), the result appears negative â€” this is positive overflow. The true result exceeds the maximum positive value representable.

q: Booth's radix-4 (modified Booth's) algorithm examines how many bits at a time?
explanation: Modified Booth's (radix-4) algorithm examines 3 overlapping bits at a time (bit i+1, i, i-1), producing one partial product for every 2 bits of the multiplier, halving the number of partial products.

q: In BCD subtraction, if the result of subtracting two BCD digits is negative (borrow needed), the correction applied is:
options: Subtract 6 (0110) from the result and borrow from the next digit
explanation: When a BCD digit subtraction results in a borrow, the correction is to subtract 6 from the result digit (equivalent to adding 10 in decimal with a borrow) to keep the digit in valid BCD range.

q: A carry select adder improves speed by:
options: Pre-computing results for both carry-in = 0 and carry-in = 1, then selecting the correct one
Using only XOR gates for addition
Using carry lookahead for all bits simultaneously
explanation: A carry select adder pre-computes two sets of sum/carry results for each group (one assuming carry-in = 0, one for carry-in = 1). When the actual carry arrives, a multiplexer selects the correct result â€” reducing delay.

q: The IEEE 754 standard defines NaN (Not a Number) as a value with:
explanation: NaN in IEEE 754 is encoded with all exponent bits = 1 and a non-zero mantissa. This distinguishes it from Â±âˆž (exponent all 1s, mantissa = 0). NaN results from operations like 0/0 or âˆš(âˆ’1).

q: In floating point multiplication, the exponents are:
explanation: In FP multiplication: result_exponent = exponent1 + exponent2 - bias (since each stored exponent includes the bias, adding them gives double bias, so one bias is subtracted). Significands are multiplied.

q: In floating point division, the exponents are:
explanation: In FP division: result_exponent = exponent1 - exponent2 + bias. Subtracting the biased exponents removes one bias, so the bias must be added back to get the correct biased result exponent.

q: Catastrophic cancellation in floating point arithmetic refers to:
options: Significant loss of precision when subtracting nearly equal numbers
explanation: Catastrophic cancellation occurs when subtracting two nearly equal floating point numbers, causing most significant bits to cancel out and leaving only the low-order (less accurate) bits, drastically reducing relative precision.

q: In a 4-bit 2's complement system, what is the result of adding 0111 (7) + 0001 (1)?
explanation: 0111 + 0001 = 1000. In 4-bit 2's complement, 1000 represents -8, not +8. The true result (+8) exceeds the max (+7), so positive overflow has occurred. The answer is 1000 with overflow.

q: The generate (G) and propagate (P) signals in carry lookahead are combined as: C_i+1 =
explanation: The carry lookahead equation is C_(i+1) = G_i + P_i Â· C_i. A carry is generated if G_i = 1 (both bits are 1), or propagated if P_i = 1 and there is an incoming carry C_i.

q: In saturating signed 4-bit arithmetic, what is 6 + 4 (with saturation)?
explanation: 4-bit signed max is +7. 6 + 4 = 10, which overflows. In saturating arithmetic, the result is clamped to +7 (maximum positive value) instead of wrapping around to -6.

q: Which operation in Booth's algorithm is performed when the bit pair (current bit, previous bit) is '00' or '11'?
options: No arithmetic operation; only arithmetic right shift
explanation: Bit pairs '00' (no transition in 0s) and '11' (no transition in 1s) indicate the middle of a run â€” no addition or subtraction is needed. Only an arithmetic right shift of the accumulator and multiplier is performed.

q: The round bit and guard bit together in floating point arithmetic provide how many extra bits of precision during intermediate computations?
explanation: Guard bit and round bit together provide 2 extra bits. Combined with the sticky bit (which is 1 bit but represents all remaining bits), IEEE 754 uses three extra bits (guard, round, sticky) for rounding decisions. The question asks about guard + round specifically = 2 bits.

q: In a Symmetric Multiprocessor (SMP) system, all processors share:
options: B. A single operating system and common memory
explanation: In SMP, all processors are identical, share the same memory, run under a single OS, and have equal access to I/O devices. This distinguishes it from NUMA or distributed memory systems.

q: Which multiprocessor architecture assigns different memory access times to processors depending on the memory location?
explanation: NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) assigns each processor a local memory that it accesses faster, while remote memory accesses (to other processors' local memory) are slower, unlike UMA/SMP where all memory access times are equal.

q: Cache coherence in multiprocessor systems ensures that:
options: A. All caches have the same size
B. Multiple copies of a memory block in different caches remain consistent
C. Only one processor can access memory at a time
D. Cache lines are never invalidated
explanation: Cache coherence guarantees that when multiple processors have copies of the same memory block cached, all copies reflect the most recent write. Without this, different processors may see stale or inconsistent values.

q: In the MESI cache coherence protocol, what does 'E' stand for?
explanation: MESI stands for Modified, Exclusive, Shared, and Invalid. 'Exclusive' means the cache line is present only in this cache and matches main memory (clean, not yet written).

q: In the MESI protocol, a cache line in the 'Modified' state means:
options: A. It is shared with other caches and up-to-date
B. It has been written and differs from main memory; only this cache has it
C. It is invalid and must be fetched from memory
D. It is read-only and shared
explanation: A 'Modified' line has been written by the local processor, is dirty (differs from main memory), and is the only copy. Before another processor can read it, this cache must write it back to memory or supply it directly.

q: Which MESI state indicates that a cache line exists in multiple caches and is consistent with main memory?
explanation: The 'Shared' state means the line is present in more than one cache and all copies match main memory. Any processor can read it, but a write must first invalidate other copies.

q: A cache line in the 'Invalid' state in MESI means:
options: A. It is the most recently updated copy
B. The data is stale or not present and must be fetched
C. No other processor has this line
explanation: An 'Invalid' cache line is unusable. The processor must fetch the latest copy from memory or another cache before it can be used. This state is set when another processor writes to the same address.

q: Which protocol is an extension of MESI that adds an 'Owned' state?
explanation: MOESI adds the 'Owned' state to MESI. An Owned line is dirty (modified) but sharedâ€”the owning cache supplies the data to other caches without writing back to main memory first, reducing memory bus traffic.

q: CPI in computer architecture stands for:
explanation: CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) measures the average number of clock cycles each instruction takes to execute. A lower CPI indicates better performance. CPU time = Instruction Count Ã— CPI Ã— Clock Cycle Time.

q: A processor executes 2Ã—10â¹ instructions with a CPI of 2 and a clock frequency of 2 GHz. What is the CPU execution time?
explanation: CPU time = IC Ã— CPI / Clock Rate = (2Ã—10â¹ Ã— 2) / (2Ã—10â¹) = 2 seconds.

q: MIPS as a performance metric stands for:
options: B. Millions of Instructions Per Second
explanation: MIPS (Millions of Instructions Per Second) measures how many millions of instructions a processor executes per second. It is calculated as Clock Rate / (CPI Ã— 10â¶) or IC / (Execution Time Ã— 10â¶).

q: MFLOPS measures:
options: A. Millions of file operations per second
B. Millions of Floating-Point Operations Per Second
C. Memory fetch latency over pipeline stages
D. Maximum logical operations per second
explanation: MFLOPS (Millions of Floating-Point Operations Per Second) measures floating-point computation throughput. It is commonly used to benchmark scientific and engineering applications. GFLOPS and TFLOPS are larger units.

q: Amdahl's Law is used to find the theoretical maximum speedup when:
options: A. All parts of a program are parallelized
B. Only a fraction of the program can be parallelized
C. Memory bandwidth is the bottleneck
D. The number of processors is unlimited
explanation: Amdahl's Law: Speedup = 1 / [(1-P) + P/N], where P is the parallelizable fraction and N is the number of processors. It shows diminishing returnsâ€”the serial portion limits the maximum speedup regardless of processor count.

q: According to Amdahl's Law, if 90% of a program is parallelizable, the maximum theoretical speedup with infinite processors is:
explanation: Maximum speedup = 1 / (1 - P) = 1 / (1 - 0.9) = 1 / 0.1 = 10. Even with infinite processors, the 10% serial portion limits speedup to 10Ã—.

q: Gustafson's Law addresses the limitation of Amdahl's Law by assuming that:
options: A. The problem size remains fixed
B. The problem size scales with the number of processors
C. Serial portions can also be parallelized
D. Memory is always the bottleneck
explanation: Gustafson's Law (scaled speedup) assumes the problem size grows with processor count, keeping parallel work constant per processor. Speedup = N - Î±(N-1), where Î± is the serial fraction. This gives a more optimistic and practical view for large-scale problems.

q: Which law gives a more optimistic speedup estimate for large parallel workloads compared to Amdahl's Law?
explanation: Gustafson's Law is more optimistic because it scales the problem size with the number of processors. In practice, larger problems often have a smaller serial fraction, so more speedup is achievable as N increases.

q: A program has a serial fraction of 0.2. Using Amdahl's Law with 4 processors, the speedup is approximately:
Dynamic power consumption in CMOS circuits is primarily proportional to:
options: B. Capacitive load, switching activity, and square of supply voltage
D. Cache size and memory bandwidth
explanation: Dynamic power P = Î± Ã— C Ã— VÂ² Ã— f, where Î± is switching activity, C is capacitive load, V is supply voltage, and f is frequency. Reducing voltage is especially effective since power scales as VÂ².

q: Static (leakage) power in modern processors is significant because:
options: A. It only occurs during computation
B. Transistors leak current even when not switching, especially at small process nodes
C. It decreases with smaller transistors
D. It is only present at high frequencies
explanation: Static power arises from leakage current through transistors even when idle. As transistor sizes shrink, leakage increases and becomes a significant fraction of total power, especially at advanced nodes like 7nm and below.

q: TDP (Thermal Design Power) in processors refers to:
options: B. The maximum sustained power the cooling system must dissipate
C. The minimum power at idle
explanation: TDP is the maximum heat a processor generates under a sustained workload, which the cooling solution must handle. It is used by system designers to size heatsinks and cooling systems, not the peak instantaneous power.

q: Which performance benchmark suite is widely used to measure CPU integer and floating-point performance across a variety of workloads?
explanation: SPEC CPU (Standard Performance Evaluation Corporation) benchmarks are industry-standard tests for CPU performance using real-world application workloads. SPECint tests integer performance; SPECfp tests floating-point performance.

q: The Linpack benchmark is primarily used to measure:
options: B. Floating-point computational performance for solving linear systems
explanation: Linpack solves a dense system of linear equations using LU decomposition. It is used to rank the TOP500 supercomputers. The result is reported in FLOPS (floating-point operations per second).

q: Which benchmark is used to evaluate transaction processing performance of databases?
explanation: TPC-C (Transaction Processing Performance Council benchmark C) simulates an order-entry environment and measures throughput in transactions per minute (tpmC). It is a standard for OLTP database systems.

q: HDDs use which mechanism to read and write data?
options: A. Electrical charge stored in floating gates
B. Magnetic heads reading/writing to rotating magnetic platters
D. Optical laser and photosensitive disc
explanation: HDDs (Hard Disk Drives) store data magnetically on spinning platters. A read/write head moves over the rotating surface to access data. Access time depends on rotational speed (RPM) and seek time.

q: SSDs are faster than HDDs primarily because they:
options: B. Have no mechanical moving parts and access data electronically
D. Have larger cache buffers only
explanation: SSDs store data in NAND flash memory chips with no moving parts. Data is accessed electronically, resulting in microsecond-level latencies compared to millisecond-level latencies for HDDs, which need to wait for platters to spin and heads to seek.

q: SATA (Serial ATA) interface has a maximum bandwidth of approximately:
explanation: SATA III (the latest SATA generation) provides a maximum bandwidth of 6 Gbps, which translates to approximately 600 MB/s after encoding overhead. This is the bottleneck for high-performance SSDs, which is why NVMe was developed.

q: NVMe (Non-Volatile Memory Express) protocol is designed to connect SSDs via which interface?
explanation: NVMe is a protocol specifically designed for SSDs connected over PCIe (PCI Express). It has much lower latency and higher throughput than SATA (up to 7+ GB/s on PCIe 4.0 x4) because PCIe provides a direct path to the CPU.

q: The primary advantage of NVMe over SATA SSDs is:
options: B. Better compatibility with older systems
C. Significantly higher bandwidth and lower latency
explanation: NVMe SSDs use PCIe lanes for direct high-speed communication with the CPU, providing 3â€“7+ GB/s bandwidth vs. ~550 MB/s for SATA, and queue depths of 65535 commands vs. 32 for AHCI/SATA, dramatically improving IOPS and latency.

q: NAND flash memory is characterized by which cell organization?
options: A. Cells connected in parallel (NOR configuration)
B. Cells connected in series (string/column configuration)
C. Cells arranged in a random access matrix
D. Cells connected to a single bitline without series strings
explanation: In NAND flash, memory cells are connected in series forming strings, which reduces the chip area needed per bit. This makes NAND denser and cheaper than NOR but requires reading entire pages and erasing entire blocks.

q: NOR flash memory is preferred over NAND flash for:
options: A. Mass storage due to higher density
B. Random byte-level read access (code execution in-place, XIP)
explanation: NOR flash allows random access at the byte level and supports Execute-In-Place (XIP), making it suitable for storing firmware and boot code. NAND is denser and cheaper but requires page-level reads, making it unsuitable for XIP.

q: SLC (Single-Level Cell) NAND stores how many bits per cell?
explanation: SLC stores 1 bit per cell using two voltage levels. It offers the best endurance (~100,000 P/E cycles), highest speed, and reliability but at the highest cost per gigabyte compared to MLC, TLC, and QLC.

q: QLC NAND flash stores how many bits per cell and what is a key trade-off?
options: C. 4 bits; lower endurance and slower write speeds
explanation: QLC (Quad-Level Cell) stores 4 bits per cell using 16 voltage levels, achieving the highest density and lowest cost per GB. However, it has the lowest endurance (~1000 P/E cycles) and slower write performance compared to SLC, MLC, and TLC.

q: Which NAND flash type provides the best balance of endurance and density, commonly used in consumer SSDs?
explanation: TLC (Triple-Level Cell) stores 3 bits per cell. It dominates consumer SSD market due to good balance of density, cost, and endurance (~3000 P/E cycles). Most modern consumer SSDs (Samsung 860/870 EVO, WD Blue) use TLC NAND.

q: Which type of bus is used to connect peripherals like keyboard and mouse in modern computers?
explanation: USB (Universal Serial Bus) is the standard interface for connecting peripherals like keyboards, mice, printers, and storage devices. USB supports hot-plugging and provides power to connected devices.

q: USB 3.0 (SuperSpeed USB) provides a maximum data transfer rate of:
explanation: USB 3.0 (also called USB 3.1 Gen 1 or USB 3.2 Gen 1) provides 5 Gbps. USB 2.0 is 480 Mbps, USB 3.1 Gen 2 is 10 Gbps, and USB4 can reach 40 Gbps.

q: DMA (Direct Memory Access) improves system performance by:
options: B. Allowing peripherals to transfer data to/from memory without CPU intervention
C. Caching frequently used data on the CPU
explanation: DMA allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to or from main memory without burdening the CPU. The CPU initiates the transfer, the DMA controller manages it, and the CPU is interrupted only upon completion, freeing it for other work.

q: In I/O communication, 'programmed I/O' (polling) differs from interrupt-driven I/O in that:
options: A. The CPU periodically checks device status rather than waiting for an interrupt
B. The device directly accesses memory without CPU involvement
C. Only one device can be connected at a time
D. It uses DMA controllers exclusively
explanation: In programmed I/O (polling), the CPU continuously checks (polls) the device's status register to see if it's ready, wasting CPU cycles. Interrupt-driven I/O lets the device interrupt the CPU when ready, freeing the CPU for other tasks.

q: An interrupt service routine (ISR) is:
options: A. A routine that schedules processes
B. A handler executed by the CPU in response to an interrupt signal from a device
explanation: When a device generates an interrupt, the CPU saves its current state, jumps to the ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) associated with that interrupt vector, services the device, then resumes the interrupted task.

q: Memory-mapped I/O differs from port-mapped (isolated) I/O in that:
options: A. I/O devices have separate address space in memory-mapped I/O
B. I/O device registers are mapped into the main memory address space, accessed with regular memory instructions
C. It requires special IN/OUT CPU instructions
D. It is used only for DMA transfers
explanation: In memory-mapped I/O, device registers share the processor's memory address space. Regular load/store instructions access them. Port-mapped I/O uses a separate I/O address space accessed by special instructions (IN/OUT on x86).

q: Which RAID level provides disk mirroring with no parity, offering full redundancy?
explanation: RAID 1 mirrors data across two or more disks. Every write goes to all disks. If one fails, data is intact on the other. It provides full redundancy but uses 50% of raw capacity.

q: RAID 5 requires a minimum of how many disks and what is the redundancy mechanism?
options: B. 3 disks; distributed parity across all disks
explanation: RAID 5 requires at least 3 disks and stripes data with distributed parity across all disks. It can tolerate one disk failure. Parity is calculated as XOR of data blocks, distributed across all disks to avoid a single parity disk bottleneck.

q: In a bus-based SMP system, bus snooping for cache coherence works by:
options: A. A central directory tracking all cache line states
B. Each cache monitoring the shared bus for memory transactions and updating its state accordingly
C. Processors polling each other for cache contents
D. The OS managing cache invalidations via software
explanation: In bus snooping, every cache controller monitors (snoops) all transactions on the shared bus. When it detects a transaction affecting a line it holds, it updates or invalidates that line, maintaining coherence without a central directory.

q: Directory-based cache coherence (used in NUMA and large-scale systems) uses:
options: A. A broadcast bus visible to all processors
B. A centralized or distributed directory tracking which caches hold copies of each memory block
D. Write-through policy for all caches
explanation: Directory-based coherence maintains a directory entry for each memory block, recording which caches have copies. Messages are sent point-to-point only to relevant caches, avoiding broadcast and scaling better than snooping for large processor counts.

q: Effective Memory Access Time (EMAT) with a cache is calculated as:
options: A. Hit rate Ã— Cache time
B. Hit rate Ã— Cache time + Miss rate Ã— Memory time
D. Miss rate Ã— Cache time + Hit rate Ã— Memory time
explanation: EMAT = h Ã— Tc + (1-h) Ã— Tm, where h is the hit rate, Tc is cache access time, and Tm is main memory access time (or Tc + Tm if cache must also be accessed on miss). This formula is fundamental to cache performance analysis.

q: The STREAM benchmark measures:
explanation: STREAM is a synthetic benchmark that measures sustainable memory bandwidth using simple vector operations (Copy, Scale, Add, Triad). It reveals the memory subsystem's bandwidth rather than CPU peak performance.

q: Which technology allows an SSD to use part of DRAM as a write buffer, significantly improving performance?
explanation: SLC caching writes incoming data first to a portion of NAND programmed as SLC (1 bit/cell) for speed, then migrates it to TLC/QLC in the background. HMB uses system DRAM for the FTL table in DRAM-less SSDs.

q: PCIe Gen 4 x4 interface (used by high-end NVMe SSDs) provides approximately how much bandwidth?
explanation: PCIe 4.0 provides 16 GT/s per lane. With x4 configuration: 4 Ã— 16 GT/s Ã— 1 byte/transfer (with 128b/130b encoding â‰ˆ 98.5% efficiency) â‰ˆ 7.9 GB/s (commonly cited as ~7 GB/s), doubling PCIe 3.0's ~3.5 GB/s x4 bandwidth.

q: Wear leveling in SSDs is used to:
options: B. Distribute writes evenly across all flash cells to extend SSD lifespan
explanation: NAND flash cells degrade with each program/erase (P/E) cycle. Wear leveling algorithms (static and dynamic) ensure that writes are distributed across all blocks so no single block is worn out prematurely, extending the SSD's usable life.

q: In the context of I/O devices, a GPU connected via PCIe is an example of which type of I/O?
options: C. DMA-based I/O with bus mastering
explanation: GPUs act as PCIe bus masters and use DMA to transfer large amounts of data between system memory and GPU memory autonomously. They initiate transfers independently without constant CPU intervention, freeing the CPU for other tasks.

q: False sharing in multiprocessor caches occurs when:
options: A. Two processors share the same physical memory
B. Two processors access different variables that happen to reside on the same cache line, causing unnecessary invalidations
C. A cache line is shared and modified by the same processor
D. The cache size is too small for the working set
explanation: False sharing happens when two processors access unrelated data (different variables) that map to the same cache line. When one processor writes its variable, the entire cache line is invalidated for the other, causing performance-degrading coherence traffic even though the logical data is independent.
